runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Recall: labor supply substitution elasticities in microsimulation (e.g., discrete/continuous labor supply modeling, marginal tax rate changes).
Recall: empirical ranges for secondary earner labor supply responsiveness to effective marginal incentives (often modest-to-moderate).
Recall: convention that substitution effects in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically below unity for many populations/earnings margins.
runs.reasoningSummary: I expect the secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity to be positive but generally modest, reflecting that participation and hours responses to changes in effective marginal incentives are present yet limited for many married/secondary earners under current-law tax-benefit environments; I therefore center the belief around ~0.6 with a right tail to ~1.2.

runs.reasoningSummary: I place most probability on a moderate elasticity: secondary earners’ labor-supply responses to net-of-tax incentives are typically weaker than for primary earners, but still meaningfully positive. I allow a wider upper tail to reflect heterogeneity (e.g., marriage/childcare composition, benefit phase-ins, and extensive-margin adjustments).
